clinical_insight: Personalized mental health interventions for autistic adults significantly outperform generic approaches when they account for sensory sensitivities, social communication differences, and alexithymia in treatment delivery. Therapists should adapt session pacing, use visual supports, offer written session summaries, and avoid implicit social demands that standard protocols assume. Co-designing treatment goals with autistic clients around self-identified priorities rather than neurotypical benchmarks improves both engagement and outcomes.
relevant_for:
- Clinical psychologists
- therapists working with autistic adults

title: MDMA-Assisted Therapy for Social Anxiety Disorder: A Randomized, Open-label, Wait-list Controlled Trial
authors: Jason Luoma, M. Kati Lear, Brian Pilecki, Jenna Lejeune, Kyong Yi, Christina Chwyl, Seth Mehr, Aryan Sarparast, Liliana Julia Kennedy, Will Lucas, Angelica Spata, Christopher Stauffer
summary: Examines the potential of MDMA-assisted psychotherapy as a treatment for **social anxiety disorder**, discussing proposed mechanisms and preliminary evidence.
clinical_insight: MDMA-assisted therapy may reduce social anxiety by lowering threat sensitivity, increasing emotional openness, and enhancing the **therapeutic alliance** during exposure to social fear memories. If preliminary evidence holds, this approach could benefit treatment-resistant social anxiety patients **who** have not responded to multiple **CBT** or pharmacotherapy trials. Clinicians should stay informed of regulatory developments and clinical trial results to appropriately counsel interested patients about benefits, risks, and evidence status.
relevant_for:
- Clinical trial therapists
- psychedelic-assisted therapy researchers

title: Dismantling the Mechanism of VR Self-Compassion Training: A Two-Session Controlled Trial with Active Controls
authors: Hongfeng Xia, Sarah Coundouris, Thomas C. Elliott, Nilufar Baghaei, Lena Oestreich, Lachlan Greig, Julie Henry, James Kirby
summary: Uses a dismantling design to identify the active components of virtual reality-based **self-compassion** interventions, determining which elements drive therapeutic change.
clinical_insight: Understanding which VR components—such as embodied perspective-taking, visual immersion, or compassionate avatar interaction—drive therapeutic change allows clinicians to adopt the most cost-effective elements of VR **self-compassion** interventions. Therapists integrating technology into practice can prioritize evidence-backed VR features while using traditional **self-compassion** exercises for the remaining components.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians incorporating virtual reality
- digital therapeutics into practice

title: Dialectical Behavior Coaching to Prevent Activist Burnout
summary: Adapts **dialectical behavior therapy** coaching principles for preventing and addressing burnout among social activists, extending **DBT** beyond traditional clinical populations.
clinical_insight: **DBT** skills such as distress tolerance, **emotion regulation**, and self-validation can be adapted into coaching formats for activists experiencing chronic burnout from sustained advocacy work. Clinicians in community and organizational settings can offer brief **DBT**-informed **psychoeducation** workshops that help activists recognize early burnout signs and apply actionable coping strategies.
relevant_for:
- Community psychologists
- organizational mental health consultants working with advocacy and nonprofit groups

title: Matching cognitive behavioural interventions to psychological processes to personalise chronic pain treatments: an adapted group Delphi study with clinical and science experts
authors: Verena Eunike Hofmann, Julia Glombiewski, Saskia Scholten
summary: Uses Delphi methodology to develop consensus on which **CBT** process strategies best match different chronic pain presentations, creating a clinical decision-making framework.
clinical_insight: Provides a consensus-based decision framework to help clinicians select the most appropriate **CBT** process strategies—such as cognitive restructuring, **behavioral activation**, or acceptance-based techniques—for specific chronic pain presentations rather than applying a one-size-fits-all protocol. Therapists can use this framework during case conceptualization to tailor interventions, potentially improving outcomes for patients **who** have not responded to standard **CBT** for pain.
relevant_for: - Pain psychologists and behavioral medicine clinicians working in multidisciplinary chronic pain clinics

title: Emotion Regulation in the Daily Lives of Women With and Without Eating Disorders: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
authors: Sarah Elizabeth Racine, Ege Bicaker, Alexia Miller, Vittoria Trolio, Lisa Yujia Zhu
summary: **Ecological momentary assessment** study of **emotion regulation** strategies in eating disorders, examining real-time associations between ER and disordered eating behaviors.
clinical_insight: Reveals real-time associations between specific **emotion regulation** strategies and disordered eating behaviors as they unfold in daily life, allowing therapists to pinpoint which ER strategies (e.g., suppression vs. reappraisal) serve as proximal triggers or buffers for bingeing or purging episodes. Clinicians can use these findings to personalize ER skills training, helping clients develop situation-specific regulation plans that target their unique antecedent patterns.
relevant_for:
- Eating disorder therapists
- registered dietitians working in outpatient or residential ED treatment

title: Barriers and facilitators to improving sleep in mental health settings: A mixed-methods systematic review of patient and professional perspectives using the Theoretical Domains Framework
authors: Alexander Scott, Ciara Kelly, Abigail Locke, Jade Campbell, Professor Susan M. Sherman
summary: Identifies systemic and individual barriers to addressing sleep problems in mental health treatment settings, proposing organizational changes to integrate sleep care.
clinical_insight: Highlights that sleep problems are frequently overlooked in mental health treatment settings due to both systemic barriers (e.g., lack of screening protocols) and individual clinician factors (e.g., low confidence in sleep interventions). Clinicians and clinic directors should advocate for routine sleep screening at intake, integrate basic sleep hygiene and **CBT**-I principles into treatment plans, and pursue training in **evidence-based** sleep interventions to address this critical **comorbidity**.
relevant_for:
- Community mental health clinicians
- clinical directors in outpatient psychiatric settings

title: Modifying Belief Updating and Paranoia with Group Based Song-Making
authors: Deanna Greco, Sandy Camilo, Santiago Castiello de Obeso, Charlotte Freeland, Anthony Pavlo, Claire Bien, Constance Lubinski, Julia Nachemson, Adam Christoferson, Joshua Kenney, Philip R. Corlett
summary: Tests whether song-making activities can facilitate belief updating in individuals with paranoid ideation, exploring creative arts-based approaches to **psychosis** treatment.
clinical_insight: Shows that creative, non-verbal approaches like song-making can facilitate belief updating in individuals with paranoid ideation, offering an alternative pathway when traditional cognitive techniques face resistance. Therapists working with **psychosis** can incorporate structured creative arts activities to indirectly challenge rigid paranoid beliefs, particularly for clients **who** struggle with direct cognitive disputation or verbal confrontation.
relevant_for: - Psychosis therapists and early intervention in psychosis (EIP) team clinicians

title: Internet-Based Self-Help Treatment for Panic Disorder with Minimal Therapist Contact: A Randomized Controlled Trial in a Spanish Clinical Population
summary: **Randomized controlled trial** of internet-based **cognitive behavioral therapy** for **panic disorder**, examining efficacy and comparing guided and unguided formats.
clinical_insight: Demonstrates that internet-based **CBT** for **panic disorder** can be effective, with guided formats likely showing superior outcomes to unguided ones. Clinicians can consider recommending guided iCBT as a scalable first-line or stepped-care option for patients with **panic disorder**, particularly those in underserved areas or **who** prefer remote treatment, while maintaining therapist oversight to maximize engagement and outcomes.
relevant_for: - CBT therapists and anxiety disorder specialists expanding into digital or telehealth service delivery

title: Difficulties in Identifying Causes of Emotions Prospectively Predict the Persistence of Suicidal Thoughts: A Six-Week Longitudinal Study
summary: Investigates how the ability to identify causes of one's emotions relates to the persistence of suicidal thoughts, with implications for emotion-focused interventions.
clinical_insight: Suggests that difficulty identifying the causes of one's emotions may contribute to the persistence of suicidal thoughts, pointing to alexithymia or emotional awareness deficits as therapeutic targets. Clinicians should assess and explicitly build clients' capacity for emotion cause identification—using skills from **emotion-focused therapy** or **dialectical behavior therapy**—as part of suicide risk interventions to help disrupt the ruminative cycles that maintain suicidal ideation.
relevant_for:
- Suicide prevention clinicians
- crisis intervention therapists working with high-risk clients

title: Daily symptom associations in prolonged grief: a dynamic network approach
authors: Huy Hoang Le, Kai Jannik Nehler, Mirjam Sophie Rueger, Qiqi Liang, Ilena Fruchi, Eva-Maria Stelzer, Clare Killikelly
summary: Applies dynamic network analysis to understand symptom interactions in prolonged grief disorder, identifying central symptoms and temporal patterns of symptom activation.
clinical_insight: Identifies central symptoms and temporal activation patterns in prolonged grief disorder through network analysis, suggesting which symptoms **act** as primary drivers of the grief network at different stages. Clinicians can prioritize targeting these bridge or central symptoms—such as yearning or identity disruption—early in treatment to produce cascading improvements across the broader symptom network, optimizing the sequencing of grief-focused interventions.
relevant_for:
- Grief counselors, bereavement therapists
- and complicated grief treatment providers

title: Breaking barriers to accessing mental healthcare for autistic people: the Autistic SPACE Framework for adult mental health settings
authors: Catherine Dakin, Daniel Davies, Bernadette Grosjean, Malcolm Kinnear, Jason Lang, Meng-Chuan Lai, Sam Porter, Anna Rebowska, Esther Sabel, Khurram Sadiq, Sebastian C. K. Shaw, Mary Doherty
summary: Introduces the SPACE Framework for supporting autistic individuals in clinical practice, providing a structured approach to neurodiversity-affirming care.
clinical_insight: Introduces a structured, neurodiversity-affirming framework (SPACE) that guides clinicians in adapting their assessment, formulation, and intervention approaches for autistic clients. Therapists can use this model to move beyond deficit-based models, ensuring sensory sensitivities, communication preferences, and social context are systematically addressed in treatment planning and session structure.
relevant_for:
- Neurodiversity-affirming therapists
- clinicians assessing or treating autistic adults in outpatient settings

title: A Proof-of-Concept Trial of a Culturally Adapted Virtual Reality-Cognitive Behavioural Therapy with Inuit in Montreal
authors: Quinta Seon, Roxanne Drainville, Liliana Gomez Cardona, Joy Outerbridge, Stéphane Bouchard, Outi Linnaranta, Martin Lepage
summary: Describes the cultural adaptation of virtual reality-enhanced **CBT** for Inuit communities, addressing cultural and logistical barriers to mental health care in remote populations.
clinical_insight: Demonstrates that VR-enhanced **CBT** can be successfully adapted for Inuit communities when cultural values, language, and local imagery are integrated into the virtual environments. Clinicians serving remote or Indigenous populations can leverage culturally adapted VR-**CBT** to overcome geographic barriers while respecting cultural context, potentially improving engagement and reducing dropout in communities with limited access to mental health services.
relevant_for:
- Telepsychology clinicians
- cultural adaptation specialists working with remote Indigenous populations

title: Turning Over a New Leaf: A Systematic Review of Nature-Based Social Prescriptions for Enhancing Psychological Wellbeing in Military and Emergency Personnel with Trauma Histories.
authors: Jeffery Leech, Lachlan Molan, Genevieve Dingle
summary: Examines the therapeutic potential of nature-based prescriptions and green care interventions for trauma recovery, reviewing evidence for mechanisms and clinical outcomes.
clinical_insight: Nature-based prescriptions offer an accessible, low-stigma adjunct to trauma-focused therapy by leveraging outdoor environments to support **affect regulation** and grounding. Therapists can collaboratively develop nature-exposure plans with clients—such as structured walks or horticultural activities—to complement traditional trauma processing work and enhance between-session regulation skills.
relevant_for:
- Trauma-focused therapists
- those working in integrated or holistic treatment settings

title: Testing predictors of change in suicide ideation among transgender and gender-diverse adults
authors: Natalia Drobotenko, Caitlin Barry, Caroline Pukall, Jeremy G. Stewart
summary: Identifies predictors of suicidal ideation in transgender adults, examining the role of minority stress, social support, and gender-affirming factors.
clinical_insight: Clinicians working with transgender adults should routinely assess minority stressors, access to gender-affirming care, and social support as part of suicide risk formulation. Affirming therapeutic stances combined with active facilitation of gender-affirming resources and community connection serve as protective factors that should be central to treatment planning.
relevant_for:
- LGBTQ+-affirming therapists
- suicide risk assessors in community and crisis settings

title: Anxiety is associated with the persistence of conditioned somatic sensations over time
authors: Hugo Alexander Fleming, Isabel Hei Wing Lau, Rukmini Chariar, Gabriel Mackie, Anna Bevan, Tim Dalgleish, Albert Powers, Camilla Nord
summary: Investigates how anxiety becomes associated with conditioned **somatic** sensations, with implications for understanding **panic disorder** and health anxiety.
clinical_insight: Recognizing that **somatic** symptoms can become conditioned triggers for anxiety helps therapists reframe **interoceptive** cues as learned associations rather than evidence of medical illness. Clinicians can integrate **interoceptive** exposure and conditioning-based **psychoeducation** into treatment for **panic disorder** and health anxiety, directly targeting the learned fear responses that maintain these conditions.
relevant_for:
- Therapists specializing in panic disorder, health anxiety
- and somatic symptom presentations

title: The Importance (and Limits) of Community Belonging as a  Protective Factor for Wellbeing in LGBTIQ+ Individuals
summary: Investigates the relationship between community belonging and mental health outcomes among LGBTIQ+ individuals, examining protective factors within minority stress frameworks.
clinical_insight: Community belonging functions as a powerful buffer against minority stress, suggesting that therapists should actively assess and facilitate clients' connections to affirming social networks as part of treatment. Clinicians can integrate resource mapping and community referral into therapy, helping LGBTIQ+ clients build supportive relationships that complement individual-level coping strategies for depression and anxiety.
relevant_for:
- LGBTQ+-affirming therapists
- mental health clinicians working with sexual and gender minority clients

title: Configuration, not Content: A Network Perspective on Gendered Prototypes of Intimate Partner Violence
authors: Hanna Feige, Timothy J. Luke, Karl Ask
summary: Applies network analysis to understand the dynamics of intimate partner violence, identifying central maintaining factors and potential intervention targets.
clinical_insight: Network analysis reveals which factors—such as jealousy, substance use, or emotional dysregulation—most centrally maintain intimate partner violence cycles, allowing therapists to prioritize intervention targets rather than addressing all problems equally. Clinicians can use these network-informed formulations to design more efficient treatment plans that disrupt the most influential pathways in the violence-maintaining system.
relevant_for:
- Domestic violence intervention specialists
- couples therapists working with relationship aggression

title: Topic shifts elicit costs in naturalistic discourse: Evidence from healthy adults and individuals with traumatic brain injury
authors: Kelly Marshall, Avery Vess, Jared Novick, L. Robert Slevc
summary: Analyzes how traumatic brain injury affects discourse production, specifically topic shifts in conversation, with implications for social communication rehabilitation.
clinical_insight: Difficulty with topic maintenance after TBI can undermine social relationships and therapy engagement, yet this communication deficit is often overlooked in standard cognitive rehabilitation. Clinicians should assess discourse coherence during intake and incorporate conversational scaffolding strategies—such as explicit topic-tracking cues and structured dialogue exercises—into rehabilitation and psychotherapy sessions.
relevant_for: - Neuropsychologists and rehabilitation therapists working with TBI patients

title: Emotion Regulation in Functional Neurological Disorder: A Multimethod Comparison of Intra- and Interpersonal Strategies
authors: Inga Niedtfeld, Johanna Hepp, Sabine Schellhaas, Astrid Steffen-Klatt, Dominik Klaasen van Husen, Valentin Held
summary: Investigates **emotion regulation** difficulties in functional neurological disorder (FND), examining how **affect regulation** strategies relate to symptom presentation.
clinical_insight: Assessing and targeting **emotion regulation** difficulties should be central to treating FND, with therapists helping patients develop adaptive strategies such as distress tolerance, affect labeling, and graduated exposure to avoided emotions. **Psychoeducation** that normalizes the link between emotional processing and neurological symptoms reduces stigma and improves treatment engagement.
relevant_for: - Neuropsychiatrists and therapists in functional neurological disorder specialty clinics

title: Agents are coming: A 5-Step Taxonomy of language-based AI-Systems for Psychiatry, Psychotherapy and Counselling
authors: Raphael Schuster, Constantin Yves Plessen, Per Carlbring, Andreas Walther
summary: Proposes an AI-driven taxonomy for classifying psychotherapy processes, using computational methods to identify common factors and therapeutic mechanisms.
clinical_insight: An AI-derived taxonomy of therapy processes can help supervisors and trainers identify which therapist behaviors and interaction patterns are most associated with good outcomes across modalities. Clinicians can use these computational frameworks to refine their case conceptualization skills, recognize effective intervention patterns, and engage in more targeted self-reflection on their in-session techniques.
relevant_for: - Psychotherapy supervisors, trainers, and process researchers

title: Safety Mechanisms and Risk Mitigation in Generative AI Mental Health Chatbots: A Systematic Scoping Review
authors: Lotenna Olisaeloka, Chris Richardson, Angel Wang, Richard Munthali, Daniel Vigo
summary: Conducts a systematic **scoping review** of safety mechanisms and risk mitigation strategies in generative AI mental health chatbots, cataloguing current approaches to crisis detection, harmful content prevention, and therapeutic boundary maintenance across commercial and research AI platforms.
clinical_insight: Clinicians considering AI chatbot adjuncts should adopt proposed safety frameworks, including clear informed consent about AI limitations, crisis escalation protocols, and regular human oversight of chatbot interactions. AI tools should be positioned as supplements to — not replacements for — therapeutic relationships, with explicit boundaries about their scope.
relevant_for:
- Digital mental health program directors
- clinicians integrating AI tools into practice

title: A Scoping Review of Service User Experiences of Potential Harm in Early Intervention in Psychosis Services in the UK
authors: Georgie Hudson, India Francis-Crossley, Craig S. Mackie, Rachel Hiller, James B. Kirkbride, Claire Powell
summary: Critically examines potential harms arising from early **psychosis** intervention, including stigma, identity disruption, and over-treatment, calling for balanced approaches.
clinical_insight: Early **psychosis** programs, while beneficial, can inadvertently stigmatize young people and disrupt identity development through labeling and intensive treatment demands. Clinicians should adopt a shared decision-making approach, carefully weighing the benefits of early intervention against risks of pathologizing normative experiences, and preserve the young person's sense of agency throughout treatment.
relevant_for: - Clinicians and case managers in early psychosis intervention services

title: Cognitive Behavioral Immersion for Depression in the Metaverse: Virtual Reality Outperforms Flat-Screens in Randomized Controlled Trial
authors: Iony Ezawa, Francisco Ramos, Steven Hollon, Gloria T. Han, Noah Robinson
summary: **Randomized controlled trial** testing virtual reality-enhanced cognitive behavioral interventions for depression, examining added benefit over standard **CBT**.
clinical_insight: Tests whether adding virtual reality environments to standard **CBT** enhances depression treatment outcomes, potentially through immersive **behavioral activation** or cognitive restructuring scenarios. If effective, clinicians could incorporate VR-based exercises—such as virtual exposure to rewarding activities or perspective-taking environments—to augment traditional **CBT**, particularly for clients **who** struggle with imaginal techniques or have limited access to real-world activation opportunities.
relevant_for: - CBT practitioners and depression treatment specialists with access to VR technology

title: Do Mindful Partners Make a Difference? A Meta-Analytic Test of the Actor-Partner Interdependence Model in Dyadic Couples
authors: Sin U Lam, William T. Hoyt, Caroline M. Swords, Siyan Gao, Alaina Srivastav, Simon Goldberg
summary: **Meta-analysis** using the actor-partner interdependence model to examine how **mindfulness** in one partner affects relationship outcomes for both partners.
clinical_insight: Shows that one partner's **mindfulness** practice can produce cross-over benefits for relationship satisfaction in the other partner, supporting the inclusion of individual **mindfulness** training within **couples therapy**. Therapists can recommend that even one partner engage in regular **mindfulness** practice as a lever for improving dyadic outcomes, or incorporate brief **mindfulness** exercises into couples sessions to strengthen both partners' relational awareness and reactivity regulation.
relevant_for: - Couples therapists and relationship counselors integrating mindfulness-based interventions

title: Ketamine’s Impact on Rumination-Related Brain Dynamics: Insights From a Randomized Controlled fMRI Trial
authors: Marvin Sören Meiering, David Weigner, Matti Gärtner, Luisa Carstens, Christian Keicher, Gerd Luippold, Andreas Wunder, Anne Weigand, Simone Grimm
summary: Uses fMRI to investigate how ketamine affects neural correlates of **rumination**, providing neurobiological evidence for ketamine's rapid antidepressant mechanism.
clinical_insight: Provides neurobiological evidence that ketamine rapidly disrupts the neural circuits underlying repetitive negative thinking (**rumination**), suggesting a mechanism beyond simple mood elevation. Clinicians overseeing ketamine-assisted therapy can leverage this by timing psychotherapeutic interventions to coincide with the post-infusion window when **rumination** pathways are most disrupted, potentially enhancing the integration of cognitive and behavioral strategies for treatment-resistant depression.
relevant_for: - Psychiatrists and psychotherapists involved in ketamine-assisted psychotherapy programs

title: The Role of Temporal Proximity in Fear Learning and Intrusive Memories during a Trauma Film Conditioning Paradigm
authors: Mikaela Richardson, Rahul Ashwin Masurkar, Lilyan Tyson, Khalisa Amir Hamzah, Gia Nhi Lam, Ottmar V. Lipp, Madeline Jarvis, Jessica Hargreaves, Ellen Barner, Casey Agassiz, Sarah Olsson, Yi Wang, Luke John Ney
summary: Investigates how temporal proximity between conditioned stimuli and unconditioned stimuli affects fear learning, with implications for **exposure therapy** timing.
clinical_insight: Demonstrates that the temporal gap between a feared cue and an aversive outcome influences the strength and persistence of fear learning, with implications for how **exposure therapy** sessions are designed. Clinicians can use these findings to optimize the timing and spacing of exposure exercises—for example, by considering whether the feared outcome is perceived as immediate or delayed—to maximize fear extinction and reduce the likelihood of fear renewal between sessions.
relevant_for: - Exposure therapists and anxiety disorder specialists using prolonged exposure or fear extinction protocols

title: Development and Validation of a Short-form (6-item) Version of the Clinician-Administered Dissociative States Scale (CADSS-SF)
summary: Develops and validates a short-form version of the Clinician-Administered Dissociative States Scale (CADSS) for efficient dissociation screening in clinical settings.
clinical_insight: Offers a validated short-form dissociation screening tool that retains the **psychometric** properties of the full CADSS while being practical for busy clinical settings. Clinicians can incorporate this brief measure into routine intakes and ongoing monitoring for clients with trauma histories, enabling faster identification of dissociative symptoms that may require targeted intervention such as grounding techniques or phase-based trauma treatment.
relevant_for: - Trauma therapists and intake assessors in community mental health and hospital-based settings

title: “Does it really count?”: Women’s Experiences of Doubt and Impostor Syndrome about an Adult Autism Diagnosis
summary: Explores the phenomenon of diagnostic doubt among adults **who** receive an autism diagnosis, examining its psychological impact and implications for post-diagnostic support.
clinical_insight: Reveals that many adults experience significant doubt following an autism diagnosis, which can undermine the very sense of identity and self-understanding the diagnosis was meant to provide. Clinicians conducting or supporting adult autism assessments should proactively address diagnostic doubt in post-diagnostic sessions, normalizing ambivalence while helping clients integrate the diagnosis into their self-narrative through **psychoeducation** and identity exploration work.
relevant_for: - Neuropsychologists and therapists conducting adult autism assessments and post-diagnostic counseling

title: Evolutionary Systems Therapy for Personality Pathology
summary: Introduces Evolutionary Systems Therapy as a novel integrative approach to personality pathology that combines evolutionary clinical psychology with systems theory, offering a framework for understanding **personality disorder** symptoms as maladaptive extreme variants of evolved social strategies.
clinical_insight: This novel integrative framework combines evolutionary psychology with systems thinking, offering clinicians a new lens for understanding maladaptive patterns as evolved strategies within relational contexts. It can enhance case conceptualization and guide interventions that work with rather than against clients' instinctive behavioral tendencies.
relevant_for:
- Integrative psychotherapists
- clinicians interested in evolutionary approaches to case formulation

title: Investigating the Effects and Mechanisms of Feedback in Psychotherapy:  A Conceptual Framework
authors: Ondřej Fajstavr, Tomáš Řiháček, Michal Čevelíček, Klára Jonášová
summary: Develops a comprehensive conceptual framework for understanding how therapist feedback influences psychotherapy processes and outcomes, synthesizing evidence on feedback timing, content, delivery method, and patient characteristics that moderate feedback effects across therapeutic orientations.
clinical_insight: Routine client feedback on progress and alliance significantly improves outcomes and helps identify clients **who** are not benefiting. Therapists should implement structured feedback tools to catch treatment failures early and adjust their approach collaboratively before clients disengage or deteriorate.
relevant_for:
- Psychotherapists across all modalities
- clinical training program directors

title: Psilocybin for Treatment-Resistant OCD: A Randomized Controlled Trial
authors: Benjamin Kelmendi, Thomas Adams, Terence Ching, Rachael Grazioplene, Stephen Kichuk, Geena Fram, Prerana Patel, Jeff Eilbott, Elizabeth D’Amico, Sarah Shnayder, Brad Martins, Calvin Bohner, Lucia Amoroso, Anastasia Jankovsky, Giuliana DePalmer, Gerard Valentine, Gabrielle Agin-Liebes, Jamila Hokanson, Christopher Pittenger
summary: Examines the relationship between mystical experiences during psilocybin sessions and outcomes in **OCD** treatment, discussing the role of altered states of consciousness.
clinical_insight: The intensity of mystical experiences during psilocybin sessions may predict **OCD** symptom improvement, suggesting clinicians facilitating psychedelic-assisted **OCD** treatment should attend to set-and-setting factors that promote meaningful psychological experiences. Integration sessions should help clients make sense of these experiences to consolidate therapeutic gains.
relevant_for:
- Psychedelic-assisted therapy researchers
- OCD treatment specialists

title: Mapping Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder: A Proof of Concept for Network-Based Case Formulation
summary: Demonstrates a proof of concept for idiographic network-based case formulation in **PTSD**, using **ecological momentary assessment** data to build personalized symptom networks grounded in the Ehlers and Clark cognitive model of **PTSD**.
clinical_insight: Building individualized symptom network formulations for **PTSD** allows therapists to identify which specific symptoms (e.g., nightmares, avoidance, hypervigilance) are most central for each patient, enabling precision targeting of treatment. Rather than applying a uniform protocol, clinicians can use idiographic network maps to decide whether to prioritize re-experiencing, avoidance, or negative cognition interventions first based on each patient's unique network structure.
relevant_for: - Trauma therapists developing personalized PTSD treatment plans

title: Single-Session Interventions for Mental Health: Umbrella Review
summary: First **umbrella review** of SSIs synthesizing 24 systematic reviews (415 unique trials) across anxiety, depression, **externalizing**, eating, and substance use problems. SSIs showed positive overall effect (SMD=-0.25), with 83% of reviews reporting significant effects.
clinical_insight: SSIs are a pragmatic tool for private practitioners to offer meaningful change in single contact, especially for waitlisted patients. Implementation research is the critical next step to determine which SSIs work in routine settings. The review does not address which formats work best for specific disorders.
relevant_for:
- Private practice clinicians
- service managers maximizing impact of brief or single-visit interventions

title: Self-efficacy in Internet-based Mental Health Interventions: A Meta-analysis
summary: **Meta-analysis** of 70 RCTs (**N = 17,407**) found moderate effects of internet-based interventions on self-efficacy, with guided interventions showing the largest effects. Self-efficacy emerged as a mediator of treatment outcomes, and lower baseline self-efficacy patients may benefit most.
clinical_insight: Supports explicitly targeting self-efficacy early in iCBT, particularly for patients with low confidence. The finding that lower self-efficacy patients benefit most is clinically important but counterintuitive. Lack of long-term follow-up on self-efficacy trajectories is a notable gap.
relevant_for: - Clinicians delivering guided internet-based interventions who want to optimize treatment mechanisms

title: Change of Threat Expectancy as Mechanism of Exposure Therapy: Evidence from 8484 Exercises of 605 Patients
summary: Using 8,484 exposure records from 605 patients across anxiety disorders, this study found that the learning rate (how effectively expectancy violation transfers into belief change) and expectancy change magnitude predicted **treatment outcome**, not violation alone.
clinical_insight: Clinicians should track whether threat beliefs actually update between sessions, not just whether patients complete exposures. Patients showing compliance without belief change may need cognitive restructuring or inhibitory learning strategies. A limitation is the naturalistic design without a control group.
relevant_for: - CBT therapists conducting exposure-based treatment for anxiety disorders optimizing learning processes

title: Understanding CBT for Psychosis Through the Predictive Coding Framework
summary: This paper integrates CBTp with predictive coding from computational neuroscience, proposing that delusions persist through failed extinction learning and inappropriate reconsolidation of delusional priors, while CBTp works by strengthening non-delusional priors **via** behavioral experiments.
clinical_insight: Bridging CBTp with predictive coding gives therapists mechanistic language for why behavioral experiments work. The framework suggests spaced retrieval of non-delusional experiences may be more important than massed exposure. The theory remains empirically untested, which is a significant blind spot.
relevant_for: - Psychologists and psychiatrists delivering CBTp who want deeper understanding of belief-update mechanisms

title: The Efficacy of Attentional Bias Modification for Anxiety: A Registered Replication
summary: This registered **replication** of Hazen et al. (2009) used a Bayesian approach to revisit ABM for **GAD**, also quantifying potential demand effects. Results add to growing evidence that ABM shows inconsistent efficacy for alleviating anxiety symptoms.
clinical_insight: This reinforces that clinicians should not divert patients from established treatments toward ABM as a standalone intervention. The debate now centers on whether ABM might work as an adjunct, which remains untested.
relevant_for: - Clinicians and researchers interested in cognitive bias modification as a complement to standard anxiety treatments

title: Neuroception of Psychological Safety Scale (NPSS): Validation in UK Adults
summary: Validated the NPSS (3-factor measure rooted in polyvagal theory) in 2,035 UK adults. Social engagement, compassion, and body sensations subscales showed acceptable-to-excellent reliability, good test-retest, and predicted correlations with **PTSD**, burnout, and wellbeing.
clinical_insight: The NPSS helps trauma therapists assess capacity for psychological safety before beginning trauma processing. Measuring safety neuroception could determine whether a patient needs more stabilization. Performance in clinical trauma populations is still needed.
relevant_for: - Trauma therapists using polyvagal-informed or phase-based approaches assessing readiness for trauma processing

title: Avatar-Based CBT/ACT Intervention for Female Orgasmic Disorder: RCT
summary: **RCT** (**N = 31**) testing avatar-based **CBT**+**ACT** intervention in Second Life for female orgasmic disorder showed significant improvements across all outcomes with 100% intervention recovery vs. 0% control at follow-up.
clinical_insight: Virtual environments may reduce barriers to discussing sexuality. The **CBT**+**ACT** integration targeting both cognitive and experiential aspects is a strength. The 100% recovery rate raises demand-characteristic concerns warranting **replication** with larger samples.
relevant_for: - Sex therapists and couples therapists working with female sexual dysfunction open to digital therapeutic tools

title: Predicting Depression and Anxiety from Temporal Rumination Features
summary: **ESM** study (4 daily assessments, 2 weeks, **N = 40**) found inertia of thought negativity predicted 67.2% of depression variance, outperforming trait **rumination**. Combined temporal and trait measures worked best. Moment-to-moment variability **did** not add predictive value.
clinical_insight: Inertia (stickiness) of negative thinking, not variability, predicts depression, supporting interventions targeting cognitive rigidity such as **behavioral activation**, cognitive defusion, or **rumination** postponement. Anxiety was better predicted by content features than temporal dynamics, suggesting different targets per disorder. Small sample warrants caution.
relevant_for: - CBT and ACT therapists treating depression who want to refine rumination interventions by targeting cognitive inertia

title: Mapping Interpersonal Emotion Regulation in Everyday Life
authors: Anh Tran, Katharine Greenaway, Joanne Kostopoulos, Sarah T. O'Brien, Elise Kalokerinos
summary: Examines how contextual factors (social setting, task demands, time of day) influence the selection and effectiveness of **emotion regulation** strategies.
clinical_insight: Shows that the effectiveness of **emotion regulation** strategies varies substantially depending on context (social setting, task demands, time of day), challenging the assumption that certain strategies are universally adaptive. Therapists should teach clients flexible ER repertoires rather than promoting a fixed set of strategies, and use functional analysis to help clients match regulation approaches to the specific situational demands they face in daily life.
relevant_for:
- Emotion-focused therapists
- DBT skills group facilitators

title: The Relationship Between Cognitive Phenotypes of Compulsivity and Impulsivity and Clinical Variables in Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
authors: Aaron Thomas Clarke , Naomi Fineberg, Luca Pellegrini, Keith R. Laws
summary: **Meta-analysis** distinguishing compulsive and impulsive dimensions in **OCD**, examining their differential relationships with symptom clusters and **treatment response**.
clinical_insight: Distinguishing compulsive from impulsive dimensions in **OCD** helps clinicians tailor treatment: compulsive-dominant presentations may respond best to traditional **ERP**, while impulsive-dominant presentations may require additional impulse control strategies, distress tolerance training, and **emotion regulation** work alongside exposure to achieve full symptom **remission**.
relevant_for: - OCD specialists and clinical psychologists conducting case formulation for treatment-resistant OCD

title: Anxiety and Compulsive Sexual Behaviour Disorder: Systematic Review
summary: Reviewed 36 quantitative studies on anxiety-CSBD relationship. Due to methodological heterogeneity, the relationship remained unclear, likely due to inconsistent CSBD measurement, limited gender diversity, and scarce longitudinal data.
clinical_insight: Clinicians should not assume anxiety drives compulsive sexual behavior or vice versa, but assess both independently. The review highlights that CSBD measurement remains problematic. Directionality cannot yet be determined.
relevant_for: - Clinicians working with CSBD or hypersexuality needing to understand the anxiety comorbidity evidence base

title: The Impact of Psychological Theory on the Treatment of  Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Adults: A Scoping Review
authors: Rebecca Champ, Marios Adamou, Barry Tolchard
summary: **Scoping review** of adult **ADHD** theory, synthesizing current understanding of **ADHD** as a lifespan neurodevelopmental condition with clinical implications.
clinical_insight: Recognizing **ADHD** as a lifespan neurodevelopmental condition rather than a childhood disorder can improve adult assessment accuracy and help clinicians provide **psychoeducation**, compensatory strategy training, and medication coordination tailored to adult functional impairments in work, relationships, and daily self-management.
relevant_for: - Adult psychiatrists and clinical psychologists assessing and managing adult ADHD

title: Reward and Punishment Learning Across Common Forms of Psychopathology
summary: Examines how reward and punishment processing abnormalities map onto different psychopathological conditions.
clinical_insight: Understanding whether a patient is more sensitive to reward or punishment can guide treatment selection — reward-hypersensitive presentations may benefit from approaches that calibrate positive reinforcement schedules, while punishment-hypersensitive patients may need graded exposure to reduce avoidance. Clinicians can use behavioral observation during assessment to identify these tendencies and tailor interventions accordingly.
relevant_for:
- Clinical neuropsychologists
- therapists conducting diagnostic case formulations across psychopathology

title: Early reduction in aversive Pavlovian bias mediates anhedonia improvement during Behavioural Activation in realistic treatment settings
authors: Lioba Berndt, Daisy Crawley, Lana Tymchyk, Anna Hall, Jakub Onysk, Tore Erdmann, Elliot Wimmer, Isabel M. Berwian, Agnes Norbury, Quentin Huys
summary: Investigates how Pavlovian approach-avoidance biases affect **behavioral activation** treatment for anhedonic depression.
clinical_insight: **Behavioral activation** for anhedonic depression may be undermined by Pavlovian avoidance biases, suggesting therapists should assess for approach-avoidance tendencies at intake. Clinicians may need to begin with very small, low-stakes positively valenced activities and use **motivational interviewing** before assigning full **behavioral activation** schedules.
relevant_for: - CBT therapists treating depression with prominent anhedonic features

title: The Role of Childhood Trauma, PTSD Symptoms and Pain Coping Strategies in Individuals with Chronic and Acute Pain: A Strength-Based Approach
authors: Norma Rosenek, Lyn Ellett, Jayne Morriss
summary: Develops and tests a comprehensive coping model for the trauma-**PTSD**-pain **comorbidity**, examining shared and distinct coping pathways.
clinical_insight: The trauma-pain coping model identifies shared and distinct pathways, indicating clinicians should address both **PTSD** and pain coping simultaneously rather than sequentially. Integrating trauma-focused interventions with pain management techniques such as paced activity scheduling, **somatic** grounding, and sleep hygiene can improve outcomes for this complex **comorbidity**.
relevant_for: - Pain psychologists and trauma therapists working with comorbid chronic pain populations
